findings.reachability: Any caller who passes attacker-controlled input to is.ip() or is.not.ip() — in the request-ip package this is HTTP request header values (e.g., X-Forwarded-For), so it is network-reachable without authentication.
findings.justification: The IPv6 regex contains the sub-expression `([\ dA-F]{1,4}:(:|\ b)|){5}` where the empty alternative `|` allows the quantifier to match 5 zero-width tokens, creating up to 2^5 ambiguous parse paths; combined with the backreference-dependent lookaheads `(?!\3)` and `(?!\2\3)` in the second half, this is a well-known ReDoS-class regex that was exploitable on Node <18 (naive NFA). V8 in Node 22.x JIT-compiles it to linear time, so exploitation requires an older runtime.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: IPv6 static regex with ambiguous quantified alternatives
detection_pattern.description: Catches static IPv6-validation regexes that repeat a group with an empty alternative `|)` 3 or more times — the classic pattern that causes polynomial backtracking in non-JIT NFA engines (Node <18, older browsers).

findings.reachability: Any caller passing null/undefined or a non-string value to is.ip(). In request-ip the value comes from request header parsing which may produce missing headers.
findings.justification: The expression `existy(value) && regexes.ipv4.test(value) || regexes.ipv6.test(value)` evaluates as `(existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value)` due to JS operator precedence — the null-guard `existy()` only protects the IPv4 branch; the IPv6 branch always executes, causing `RegExp.prototype.test(null)` to silently coerce null to the string `'null'` and test it. This is a correctness bug; the current IPv6 regex does not match `'null'`, but a future regex change could introduce a false positive.

detection_pattern.section_name: Logic: null guard bypassed by operator precedence in OR chain
detection_pattern.description: Detects patterns where a null/existy guard is `&&`-chained with one test but a second test is `||`-chained at the same level, meaning the guard does not protect the second test and null/undefined gets silently coerced to a string.
